name: Goetia Evocation
description: Goetia Evocation, a practice rooted in medieval grimoires like the Lesser Key of Solomon, involves summoning and commanding spirits, particularly demons, for specific purposes. It combines elements of Jewish mysticism, Christian demonology, and Greco-Roman magic. The practitioner, often a trained magician or scholar, prepares a ritual space, utilizing circles, triangles, and sigils to bind and control the spirit. The process involves reciting specific incantations and prayers, invoking divine names to assert authority over the summoned entity. Goetia is not inherently malevolent; spirits are often compelled to provide knowledge, perform tasks, or grant favors. The magician's success hinges on their knowledge of the spirits' names, seals, and hierarchies, as well as their ability to maintain control and adherence to the ritualistic protocols outlined in the grimoires. The cultural significance lies in its representation of humanity's attempt to understand and manipulate the unseen forces of the cosmos, reflecting a blend of fear, curiosity, and the pursuit of power. The practice has influenced literature, art, and occult movements throughout history.
subtitle: Summoning and Commanding Spirits

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Solomon's Demons
keyMyths.description: King Solomon, through divine power, bound 72 demons and forced them to build the Temple of Jerusalem. This myth provides the foundation for the authority claimed in Goetic practices.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Lucifer
keyMyths.description: Lucifer's rebellion against God and subsequent fall from grace is often invoked to explain the demonic hierarchy. Goetic rituals frequently reference this event.
